subject: science

primary.justification: The chapter's central domain is how electricity works through simple circuits to produce two distinct phenomena — the magnetic effect (compass deflection, electromagnet construction, pole determination, strength variation) and the heating effect (nichrome wire, household appliances, resistance as mechanism). Students manipulate circuit elements across six activities to demonstrate both effects, directly matching the C-2.2 specification of describing how electricity works and demonstrating its heating and magnetic effects.

A structurally distinct strand of the chapter addresses properties of magnets as applied to electromagnets: students determine the North and South poles of an electromagnet (Activity 4.4), investigate how pole reversal follows from current direction reversal, and a Step Further extension connects this to Earth behaving as a giant magnet due to liquid iron core currents. This magnet-properties investigation is independent of the circuit-manipulation strand and substantively develops C-2.3 (properties of natural and artificial magnets; Earth as a magnet).

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to describe how electricity produces magnetic and heating effects by constructing electromagnets, investigating their properties and pole behaviour, and explaining the operation of cells and batteries that supply the current.
